preFix.title: Needle-felted squirrel
preFix.subtitle: A 12 cm sitting red squirrel in rust and cream merino, with a raised bushy tail
preFix.excerpt: A sitting needle-felted squirrel in rust-orange and cream merino roving. The bushy tail arches up and over the body and acts as the main support for the seated pose. Ears have small tufts characteristic of the red squirrel. Rust body, cream chest, darker tip on the tail.
preFix.sourceNotes: Needle-felted squirrel figures are a standard project in UK fibre arts teaching. Red squirrel anatomy and tail construction synthesised from general needle felting practice; not drawn from a specific PD source.

content.text: A squirrel's large bushy tail makes it a rewarding needle felting subject. The tail on this figure arches up from the rump and curves forward over the top of the body, acting as a visual anchor for the
sitting pose
and providing structural support when the figure is displayed. The body is rust-orange
roving
with a cream chest worked in as
inlay
. The tail is rust at the base and darker (burnt umber or dark rust) at the tip. Small ear tufts are a key detail of the red squirrel and are added last. A
felting needle
in 36-gauge handles bulk shaping; 38-gauge handles detail; 40-gauge (optional) for surface smoothing.

attrs.body: Always stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. The needle must enter foam, not a hard surface, on every stroke.
attrs.title: Needle safety

items.name: Merino roving, cream or white, for chest inlay
Merino roving, dark rust or burnt umber, for tail tip
Foam felting mat, at least 10 cm thick

content.text: Method
Step 1: Form the body

Roll a generous amount of rust-orange roving into a teardrop shape: wider and rounder at the haunches, narrowing toward the neck. Needle on the foam mat with the 36-gauge needle, rotating constantly, until the body is firm and measures about 7 cm tall and 5 cm wide at the haunches. The underside should be flat so the squirrel sits without rocking. Once the body is firm, add a thin wisp of cream roving across the front chest area and needle in using the inlay technique to create the pale chest.
Step 2: Build the tail

The tail is the largest component and is built in two stages. First, roll a thick rope of rust-orange roving about 12 cm long and 4 cm wide when unfelted. Needle into an elongated oval, flat on one side and rounded on the other, about 10 cm long and 5 cm wide. This is the main tail mass. To make the tail fluffy, tease loose wisps of roving over the outer (rounded) surface and needle them in very shallowly so they stand up slightly rather than lying flat. Add darker roving at the tip using the inlay technique.
Step 3: Attach the tail to the body

Hold the flat side of the tail against the back of the body, with the tail base at the rump and the tip arching up and forward over the back of the head. Needle through the flat side of the tail into the body repeatedly along the full length of the join. The tail should make contact with the body from the rump nearly to the shoulder, so the join area is long and the tail is well secured. Once attached, check the arch: the tip of the tail should sit just above the head.
Step 4: Form the head

Roll a small amount of rust-orange roving into a rounded oval about 5 cm long and 4 cm wide. Needle on the mat until firm. The squirrel's head should have a slightly pointed muzzle at the front: needle the front of the head more on the lower face to draw it slightly forward. Add a small dot of dark roving for the nose at the muzzle tip.
Step 5: Make and attach the ears with tufts

For each ear, needle a small pointed triangular shape from rust roving on the mat: about 2 cm tall and 1.5 cm wide. Once each ear is firm, leave the base edge loose for attaching. Position the ears at the top of the head, slightly to the sides. Needle the base edge firmly into the head. Then add a tiny tuft to each ear: pull a very small pinch of dark roving and needle it at the tip of each ear with just a few strokes to create a protruding point. This detail is the signature of the red squirrel.
Step 6: Attach the head and add front paws

Hold the head against the top of the body and needle through the neck join from all angles. Add a small amount of roving to fill any gap at the join and needle in. For the front paws, roll two small oval shapes of rust roving (each about 2.5 cm long) and needle until firm. Attach at the lower front of the body, positioned as if the squirrel is holding something. Needle through each paw into the body from above and from the side.
Step 7: Surface finish

Work over the full figure with the 40-gauge needle (or 38-gauge) using light, shallow strokes to smooth loose surface fibres. Do not over-smooth the tail: some texture on the tail surface gives it a more convincing furry appearance.

items.fix: Detach the tail, needle it more firmly on the mat until it holds its shape when held in the air, then re-attach. If it is still too heavy, reduce the roving and rebuild with a thinner tail.
items.cause: The tail was not needled firmly enough before attachment, or it is too heavy
items.problem: The tail is sagging and not holding its arch over the body

items.fix: Add a tiny pinch of fresh roving to the tip and needle with only 2-3 strokes, enough to lock the fibres at the base but leaving the tip free.
items.cause: Too many needle strokes were applied to the tuft, pushing it flat
items.problem: The ear tufts are bent flat rather than pointing upward

items.fix: Add a fresh layer of cream roving over the chest area and needle only shallowly (5 mm depth) with a 40-gauge needle.
items.cause: The inlay was needled too deeply
items.problem: The cream chest inlay has turned orange from the body colour bleeding through

content.text: Care and finishing
Spot-clean with a barely damp cloth. Keep away from moisture. Display on a shelf or in a shadow box. The ear tufts are the most delicate detail: avoid pressing on the ears during handling.
